topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The studio air hung thick and stagnant, mirroring the heavy feeling in Elias’s chest. He stared at the canvas, a sprawling landscape rendered in bruised purples and charcoal greys, and felt nothing but a dull ache behind his eyes. He was supposed to be energized by the creation, but all he felt was a profound inertia. Then, his gaze snagged on a detail near the horizon line—a delicate, almost hidden line of intricate script that wasn't part of his composition. It was a tattoo. A tiny, perfectly executed piece of body art embedded within the very fabric of his work. A slow, cold dread began to coil in his stomach. He leaned closer, his breath catching, the quiet hum of the ventilation system suddenly deafening. Who had done this? And why?

[story 2]

Maya dragged herself to the easel, the floorboards protesting beneath her weight with every reluctant movement. Her shoulders were slumped, a physical weight pressing down on her neck. The colors she’d chosen—a jarring clash of electric blue and burnt orange—felt hollow, reflecting the emptiness inside her. As she stepped back to assess the piece, her eyes caught the anomaly. Beneath the swirling vortex of paint, etched almost invisibly into the underlayer, was a stylized depiction of a raven in flight. It was undeniably a tattoo. A sharp, unwelcome prickle ran down her spine, instantly stealing the meager energy she had managed to gather. She traced the outline with a hesitant finger, a tight knot forming in her throat. Someone had marked her work.

[story 3]

Arthur meticulously wiped a smear of cadmium red from his palette, the motion slow and deliberate, devoid of any spark of creativity. The silence of the loft amplified the low thrum of dissatisfaction vibrating through him. He felt profoundly disconnected from the world, his limbs heavy and unresponsive. While cleaning brushes, his attention drifted to the central figure of his latest portrait—a haunting study in shadow. There, overlaid on the texture of the skin, was a dark, flowing pattern, unmistakably inked. It was a tattoo. A sudden, sharp intake of breath escaped him. He felt a strange mixture of anger and profound unease bloom in his core. Someone had claimed a piece of his vision.
